retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- Artifact
retrieved_docs.content: An artifact on an image is a feature that does not correlate with the physical properties of the subject being imaged and may confound or obscure the interpretation of that image. Because the mechanism for image creation is different between flat-panel detectors and computed radiography, the causes and appearances of some artifacts can be unique to these different modalities. Artifacts can occur for several reasons; examples include damage to X-ray detectors, liquid contamination of X-ray detectors, backscatter radiation detection, and image saturation. The typical appearances of the aforementioned artifacts on chest radiography are listed below. [17] Damage to X-ray detectors: White banding that can progressively grow over time Liquid contamination: Repeated vertical banding Backscatter radiation: Shadow image of superimposed digital detector electronics Image saturation: Clipping of anatomy

retrieved_docs.title: Internal Tooth Whitening -- Preparation -- Patient Consent
retrieved_docs.content: Although rare, external cervical resorption is a noteworthy complication associated with internal tooth whitening. Studies on the etiology of external cervical resorption primarily attribute its development to orthodontic treatment (24.1%), surgical procedures (5.1%), and internal bleaching (3.9%). [8] External cervical resorption is usually asymptomatic and discovered incidentally on radiographic findings. Resorption appears as a radiolucent, dish-shaped near the cementoenamel junction (CEJ) on x-rays. Clinically, the affected tooth may respond well to sensitivity testing and bleed profusely upon probing. Additional clinical findings may include tenderness on percussion and swelling of the interdental papillae. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Bullous Emphysema -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with suspected COPD undergo plain chest radiographs, which often reveal a flattened diaphragm because of hyperinflation. Bullae appear as radiolucent areas surrounded by thin, curved, white, or light-colored lines representing interlobar fissures. As the disease progresses, prominent hilar vascular markings and cardiomegaly may be evident. [22] Computed tomography (CT) scan of the chest is more sensitive for detecting COPD than chest radiography. A CT scan of the chest is useful when clinicians suspect a complication of COPD or an alternative diagnosis like pneumonia, pneumothorax, giant bullae, thromboembolic disease, or lung cancer, or if a lung cancer screen is appropriate. [23] [24] [25] Giant bullae are typically evident on chest radiographs. However, they may be difficult to differentiate from a pneumothorax. A CT scan of the chest is helpful in these circumstances.

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Healthy gingival tissue looks pink or pigmented in dark-skinned patients, firm, with no signs of redness or swelling, and with no bleeding after gently passing a periodontal probe along the gingival crevice. On periodontal probing, healthy gingiva shows less than 3 mm crevices, and there is no bone loss on x-rays.In many instances, gingivitis may go unnoticed by the patient as the disease may exist and progress without any symptoms. When symptomatic, the patient usually gives a history of bleeding from the gingiva while brushing, flossing, and sometimes eating particularly hard food, along with halitosis that does not resolve even after performing oral hygiene. Physical examination of the oral cavity reveals the presence of an inflamed and tender gingiva that usually bleeds on gentle probing. The gingival margins that show a knife-edge appearance and the gingival tissue with a stippled aspect found in healthy gingiva are replaced by a more rounded and shiny aspect. Significant plaque and calculus deposits are usually seen. In chronic gingivitis, the size of the gingival tissue may be increased towards the incisal due to edema or hyperplasia, resulting in probing depths more than 3 mm; however, no attachment loss has occurred. These are known as false pockets.

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Imaging -- Plain Films
retrieved_docs.content: X-rays, also known as plain radiography, are a widely used imaging technique that uses ionizing radiation with wavelengths of less than 10 nm. [5] These rays are emitted from a generator and pass through the body, interacting with tissues differently. The x-ray beam is either scattered, absorbed, or transmitted through the patient’s tissues, and a detector collects this data to form an image. [5] The resulting image is a negative representation of what was detected by the detector: areas that absorb or scatter the x-rays (such as bones) appear bright white. In contrast, areas that transmit the x-rays (such as air) appear black. Radio-opaque objects, like bones, block x-rays and thus appear white, whereas radiolucent objects, like air, absorb or transmit the rays, appearing black on the film.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Evaluation -- Plain Film Radiograph
retrieved_docs.content: All patients should have orthogonal plain film radiographs when identifying potential bone cancer. Plain x-rays may show the following findings: Osteolytic, osteoblastic, or mixed changes A moth-eaten appearance (suggests bone destruction secondary to a rapidly expanding tumor within a bone, commonly seen in Ewing sarcoma and telangiectatic osteosarcoma) A permeative appearance (suggests the tumor progresses through bone, with an ill-defined zone between the tumor and healthy bone, often seen in small cell tumors, including Ewing sarcoma) "Onion skinning," with the tumor lifting partially-formed periosteal bone (classically seen in Ewing sarcoma) "Codman triangle," with periosteum lifted off bone and osteoid laid down "Sunburst" appearance, with vertical osteoid calcification due to significant periostitis [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiology, Image Production and Evaluation -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There are several things to consider when evaluating an image. The contrast of an image gets produced by the difference in tissue density displayed on a radiograph, and it can thus be manipulated to distinguish an area of interest. Contrast is dependent on the film density, film processing, curve of the film, and the use of intensifying screens. The density is the amount of light transmitted through the film and measures the degree of film darkening. Distortion of the image can depend on the size, shape, and position of the target structure within the body. Identification markers serve to differentiate the location, type of image, position, and orientation of the image. These markers are necessary to identify and analyze the anatomical structures correctly. Awareness of these factors is essential when evaluating radiographic images. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Definition/Introduction -- Dynamic Range
retrieved_docs.content: The dynamic range is the range of various X-ray intensities the detector can image. [3] Radiographic detectors that provide good contrast over a wide dynamic range are essential for obtaining high-quality digital radiographs. The detectors with wide dynamic range show very low or very high exposure values in an image, and viewers can view the range of different visible intensities. Although narrow latitude images show greater visible contrast, the extreme exposure intensities would appear too white or black with no discernible contrast.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteonecrosis Imaging -- Plain Films
retrieved_docs.content: Early findings may show slight osteopenia in comparison to the opposite bone. In small bones, osteonecrosis will appear as areas with increased hardness compared to nearby bones. In long bones, characteristic imaging findings from early to chronic stages include scattered areas of hollowness surrounded by a winding rim of hardness. This winding hard rim is most noticeable in the areas between the metaphysis and diaphysis

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Clinical Significance -- Penetration
retrieved_docs.content: Suppose there is over-penetration of a chest radiograph. In that case, more X-ray beams are hitting the photosensitive detector, and the radiograph looks darker than it normally would, especially in the lung fields, making it appear more radiolucent than it should. This decreases lung markings and can raise suspicion for pathologies such as emphysema or even pneumothorax in a patient who may be completely healthy. [23] Ways in which to avoid making mistakes include making a conscious effort to check for adequate penetration, adjusting the window if possible, checking lateral views if available, and assessing for other imaging findings you should see in a suspected pathology (eg, flattening of the diaphragm in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.)

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Diagnostic Testing -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: In dentistry, transillumination refers to the transmission of light through dental tissues to aid in caries diagnosis. However, its uses are much broader, including evaluating developmental defects, like dental fluorosis, root canal orifices, and tooth fractures and cracks. [13] Fiber-optic transillumination supplements clinical examination, with specificity and sensitivity as high or even higher than radiographs, while avoiding exposing patients to radiation. [14] Fiber optic transillumination devices must be small, with apertures of 3 mm or less to give a point source and be compact. Although used by some providers, dental curing lights are not recommended for transilluminating teeth since they are associated with a risk of macular degeneration and retinal injury. [13] Sound dental tissues have a different light transmission index than caries, calculus, external tooth discolorations, and restorative materials, making it possible to distinguish such changes with fiber optic transillumination. A carious lesion looks like a shadow within the dental structure because it has a lower light transmission index than normal tissue. Calculus appears as a darker area on the tooth surface. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Internal Tooth Whitening -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth discoloration is a common complaint among patients seeking to improve the aesthetics of their anterior teeth. Tooth discoloration or stains are described as a change in color or translucency of 1 or more teeth. Depending on the etiology and location, tooth stains can be extrinsic or intrinsic. Understanding the specific etiology is crucial for determining the most appropriate treatment plan.

retrieved_docs.title: Stress Reaction and Fractures -- Evaluation -- Plain Radiographs
retrieved_docs.content: Plain radiographs are typically the first imaging modality for suspected stress injuries because they are widely available, affordable, and involve low radiation exposure. However, they frequently appear normal early in the injury process, resulting in low sensitivity. [11] Subtle signs—such as periosteal elevation, cortical thickening, sclerosis, or a fracture line—often take 2 to 3 weeks to become visible. A "dreaded black line" may signal a high-risk fracture in areas like the tibia or femur. [36] [37] Spondylolysis poses an additional diagnostic challenge, sometimes requiring 5 specialized views to visualize the pars interarticularis properly. [34] Even with multiple views, early lesions can be missed, so further imaging, particularly MRI, is warranted if clinical suspicion remains high despite normal radiographs.

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to obtaining a comprehensive history, several diagnostic tools can aid in elucidating the diagnosis. A thorough visual examination with magnification and illumination may reveal fracture lines and communication with existing restorations. Fibreoptic transillumination (FOTI) is particularly valuable for investigating cracked teeth. Allowing light to penetrate the tooth until deflected by a fracture produces a visible transition line from light to dark, highlighting the presence of cracks. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Digital Radiography - The computed radiographic image receptor, an elementary form of a digital detector, is similar yet different from screen-film imaging in a few ways. One similarity is their mutual use of X-ray-sensitive plates. Computed radiography using storage phosphors uses trapped electrons in metastable states to produce an image instead of a scintillator used in screen-film radiography. Metastable electrons are released with a stimulating laser beam, with the emission of light on the return of these electrons to a ground state. [11] This light emission is used to establish an image. Computed radiography also comparatively has a reduced radiation dose and better contrast resolution when compared to screen radiography (although the resolution is worse in digital radiography). Flat Panel Detectors are classically associated with digital detectors. It is faster than film radiography and includes direct and indirect systems. Explained simply, indirect systems require a latent image to be released as a light, then converted to a charge and a digital image. In contrast, direct systems directly convert x-rays into charge (and subsequently an image) without this intermediate step. Indirect systems employ a cesium iodide scintillator, which constitutes most flat-panel detectors, while direct systems employ amorphous selenium.

retrieved_docs.title: Ankylosing Spondylitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Throughout AS, a series of distinct radiographic changes characteristics can progressively develop. In the early stages, a notable sign is the "squaring" of vertebral bodies, which is best visualized on lateral X-rays. This squaring occurs due to inflammation and bone deposition, resulting in the loss of normal concavity of the anterior and posterior borders of the vertebral body. Additionally, early-stage radiographs may reveal Romanus lesions, also known as "shiny corner signs," characterized by small erosions and reactive sclerosis at the corners of the vertebral bodies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2609
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Intervention Terminology encompasses the concepts الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر (chest X‑ray) and عملية صدرية (chest surgery). Within this framework, the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر, indicating that chest radiography is a key element of the symptom complex. Together, these relationships highlight that the terminology set integrates both diagnostic imaging of the chest and surgical interventions, providing clinicians with a cohesive reference for linking symptom presentations to appropriate chest‑focused diagnostic and therapeutic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with the الأَشعة السينية على الصَدْر, indicating that this particular pattern of signs and symptoms often correlates with findings observed on a chest X‑ray. This relationship guides clinicians to employ chest radiography when the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is present, facilitating the evaluation of underlying thoracic pathology and informing subsequent patient care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest revealing a miliary pulmonary infiltrate is a radiologic finding that occurs in the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: forms of إجراء علاجي. Diagnostic imaging, specifically الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر (chest X‑ray), is clinically associated with the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX, highlighting its role in patient assessment. Together, these entities and relationships form an integrated network of therapeutic, diagnostic, and research procedures essential for accurate documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 383
retrieved_docs.content: how a simple chest X‑ray can serve as the gateway to a hierarchy of imaging techniques—from plain radiography to advanced CT of multiple body regions—while also connecting to specific disease associations and broader neuro‑imaging frameworks essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both القلق (anxiety) and with الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية (abnormal chest X‑ray). Anxiety can amplify the perception of chest discomfort, while an abnormal chest X‑ray may reveal underlying pulmonary or cardiac pathology that also manifests as nonspecific chest pain. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may reflect both a psychological factor (القلق) and a radiographic abnormality (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) is essential for a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are specific types of chest CT scans. Diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material, as well as diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material followed by contrast material(s) and additional sections, represent narrower sub‑categories within the broader chest CT imaging modality. In addition, computed tomography of the chest is used as a finding method for detecting miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure of chest CT techniques and illustrate how particular CT protocols are applied for detailed diagnostic evaluation of thoracic pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 464
retrieved_docs.content: and are placed within a broader category of Unspecified examination, highlighting that such encounters may be documented under a general examination heading when the specific imaging modality is not further detailed. Overall, this network illustrates how routine chest radiography and specific clinical symptom complexes act as triggers for a variety of targeted CT examinations, each categorized under the generic concept of computerized tomographic imaging, thereby guiding diagnostic pathways and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 5 -- Community 2932
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with abnormal findings on a chest X‑ray and with aortic stenosis (aortic narrowing). This relationship indicates that when a patient presents with nonspecific chest pain, clinicians should consider obtaining a chest radiograph to look for any abnormalities and should also evaluate the aorta for possible stenotic lesions, as both radiographic abnormalities and aortic narrowing can be underlying contributors to the pain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 291
retrieved_docs.content: Tomografie is recognized as a medical diagnostic imaging modality; it is classified within the broader category of plain X‑ray techniques and is considered a specific type of medical diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are specialized forms of a ct scan chest, representing specific imaging techniques that use contrast to evaluate thoracic structures. A ct scan chest serves as the imaging modality for identifying thoracic pathology, and when a computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum is performed, the finding is obtained through the ct scan chest method. Thus, chest CT, including its contrast‑enhanced angiographic and axial tomography variants, is the primary diagnostic tool for visualizing and characterizing a mass of the mid mediastinum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission imaging is linked to صورة مقطعية محوسبة, indicating that this imaging modality encompasses computed tomography scans. In clinical practice, صورة مقطعية محوسبة frequently co‑occurs with صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية, reflecting that CT imaging is often performed together with spinal‑region myelographic or spinal cord imaging to provide complementary anatomical detail for diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1696
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast is classified as a type of CT scan chest, indicating that it is a specific chest computed tomography procedure that uses contrast material to visualize thoracic vasculature. In a similar manner, the diagnostic thoracic computed tomography protocol described as “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” is also related to CT scan chest, but it is noted to have a narrower relationship, meaning it represents a more detailed or specialized imaging sequence within the broader category of chest CT. Together, these entities illustrate that chest CT imaging encompasses a range of techniques—from general diagnostic scans to contrast‑enhanced angiographic studies—each tailored to particular clinical questions about thoracic anatomy and vascular pathology.
